FINS3646: Toolkit for Finance Week 1: Lecture

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

The team

Lecturer-in-charge  Robert Tumarkin Developers  Breno Schmidt  Robert Tumarkin Contact:  Preferred: Post a message on ed, either private or public, or  Less preferred: Email me at r.tumarkin@unsw.edu.au

Overview

Course introduction A brief intro to Python & PyCharm Classes and instances

Course introduction

This course builds your skills in analysing and working with financial data using Python and AI.

Coding with AI:  Settings for which AI works well and its limits  Why testing is essential  Best practices

This course builds your skills in analysing and working with financial data using Python and AI.

Coding with AI:  Settings for which AI works well and its limits  Why testing is essential  Best practices Running event studies in Finance

We will teach as if you are completely new to programming

This is not a Computer Science course  Our focus is on intuition and application

This is not a Computer Science course  Our focus is on intuition and application This is not “principles of programming”  Most of you do not plan to become Python developers, instead you will use AI as a tool to help you write code

This is not a Computer Science course  Our focus is on intuition and application This is not “principles of programming”  Most of you do not plan to become Python developers, instead you will use AI as a tool to help you write code  There are many essential topics we will not cover (e.g., creating your own classes, design patterns)

Our goal is to give you just enough Python to use AI effectively and solve real problems in financial research and analysis

Pre-recorded Lessons

1. Introduction to Python  Introducing Python  Classes, instances, and objects – Intuition  Python basics 2. Building blocks  Constructors, literals, basic types  Identifiers and names  Assignment statements  Rules and conventions 3. Data structures  Data structures: Lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries  Data structures and references 4. Control flow  Boolean logic and conditional execution  Control flow – Loops (Parts I and II)  Comprehensions  Control flow – Functions 5. Modules  Modules and packages, docstrings  IO and paths 6. Namespaces and scope 7. Event studies in Finance 8. Pandas (Parts I and II)

This is not our weekly schedule. Please refer to ed to find out what will be discussed each week.

A brief introduction to Python

Your brain can answer this instantly. A computer can also calculate 1 2,  But it only understands machine code (sequences of 0s and 1s).

Your brain can answer this instantly. A computer can also calculate 1 2,  But it only understands machine code (sequences of 0s and 1s). Since we don’t “speak” machine code, we need a translator.  A programming language allows us to communicate with that translator.

Your brain can answer this instantly. A computer can also calculate 1 2,  But it only understands machine code (sequences of 0s and 1s). Since we don’t “speak” machine code, we need a translator.  A programming language allows us to communicate with that translator.

Intuition:

For our purposes, we can think of Python as both: A programming language that lets us write instructions clearly. A translator program, called the Python interpreter, that runs those instructions.

For our purposes, we can think of Python as both: A programming language that lets us write instructions clearly. A translator program, called the Python interpreter, that runs those instructions.

For our purposes, we can think of Python as both: A programming language that lets us write instructions clearly. A translator program, called the Python interpreter, that runs those instructions.

Note: In reality this process is more complex. The Python interpreter first compiles your code into “bytecode” (not machine code). Then the Python Virtual Machine translates that bytecode into machine-level operations.

In this course, we will focus on how we communicate with Python—not how Python communicates with the CPU.

Using PyCharm to communicate with Python

PyCharm puts this workflow together, so you can easily send instructions to the Python interpreter.

Below is a list of topics we discussed in class

We asked ChatGPT the following question:  If I want to use AI to help me code in Python, do I really need to learn Python at all?

Below is a list of topics we discussed in class

We asked ChatGPT the following question:  If I want to use AI to help me code in Python, do I really need to learn Python at all?  Ask ChatGPT: Can you help me write an email asking for details about a job position at a bank in Hong Kong?  I argued that before sending the email we must: 1. Understand the email 2. “Test” the email (by reading it) to ensure it communicates what we want  In the same way, when using AI to write code we must: • Understand the basics of the programming language • Test the code to confirm it behaves as intended

Classes and instances - Intuition  Classes and instances - Intuition

Python is an object-oriented language. It is important that you start you Fintech journey by understanding this concept intuitively.

Consider the iPhones in the room – these are physical objects manufactured by a factory.

Consider the iPhones in the room – these are physical objects manufactured by a factory.

iPhone

Consider the iPhones in the room – these are physical objects manufactured by a factory.

iPhone

We will say these factories produce a specific class of physical objects, and represent them in yellow boxes.

Our actual, physical phones are objects – distinct instances of an iPhone.

iPhone

Our actual, physical phones are objects – distinct instances of an iPhone.

Instance (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device : iPhone iPhone

students_device : iPhone

Our actual, physical phones are objects – distinct instances of an iPhone.

Instance (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device : iPhone iPhone

students_device : iPhone

We will represent these physical instances (objects) using gray boxes, noting the class (factory) that created them.

Instance (Object) Notation: <identifier> : <class>

Instance (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device : iPhone iPhone

students_device : iPhone

<identifier> – a unique object name <class> – the factory that produced the object

Instances are specific physical objects.

Classes are factories that produce objects.

Every instance MUST belong to a class.

Physical objects produced by a factory share common intrinsic characteristics (attributes).

Instances (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device: iPhone iPhone

.owner : Person .appstore_id: UserId

Similarly, instances that belong to the same class share common attributes.

Physical objects produced by a factory share common intrinsic characteristics (attributes).

Instances (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device: iPhone iPhone

.owner : Person .appstore_id: UserId

We will write attributes using a leading dot (decimal point)

While we take note of the attributes produced in the factory, each manufactured object must have the attribute.

Instances (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device: iPhone iPhone

.owner: Person = Lecturer .owner : Person .appstore_id: UserId = abc .appstore_id: UserId

Every attribute has a value specific to every instance.

Attribute Notation: .<attribute>: <type> = <value>

Instances (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device: iPhone iPhone

.owner: Person = Lecturer .owner : Person .appstore_id: UserId = abc .appstore_id: UserId

students_device: iPhone

<attribute> – the name of the attribute <type> – the kind of thing the attribute describes <value> – the value of the attribute for that specific instance

<type> is the class of the attribute. We use this term to distinguish the type of information stored in the object’s attribute from the class of the object itself.

Instances and classes:  Instances are specific physical objects.  Classes are conceptual categories.  Every instance MUST belong to a class. Attributes:  Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.  The attributes define what it is to be a class.

Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.

Actions can require just a single object. . . students_phone.check_time() : Time

. . . or may multiple objects students_phone.call(friends_phone) : Conversation

students_phone.purchase_coffee(cafe) : Flat_White

Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.

Actions can require just a single object. . . students_phone.check_time() : Time

. . . or may multiple objects students_phone.call(friends_phone) : Conversation

students_phone.purchase_coffee(cafe) : Flat_White

We will use the term method to refer to actions one can perform with objects.

Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.

Methods need not accomplish anything. . . students_phone.admire_phone() : None students_phone.tiktok() : None

Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.

Methods need not accomplish anything. . . students_phone.admire_phone() : None students_phone.tiktok() : None

We signify that the the method didn’t produce a result object with the word None.

Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.

Instances (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device: iPhone iPhone

.owner: Person = Lecturer .owner : Person .appstore_id: UserId = abc .appstore_id: UserId

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None

Method Notation: .<method>(parameters) : <type>

<method> – the name of the action <parameters> – other objects required to perform the method  Any other objects required by the action are listed in parentheses (other1, other2...)  Empty parentheses () are used if the action doesn’t require other objects. <type> – the type of object that results from performing the action

Method Notation: .<method>(parameters) : <type>

<method> – the name of the action <parameters> – other objects required to perform the method  Any other objects required by the action are listed in parentheses (other1, other2...)  Empty parentheses () are used if the action doesn’t require other objects. <type> – the type of object that results from performing the action

<type> is the class of the object that results from the action. We use this term to distinguish it from the class of the object itself.

Method Notation: .<method>(parameters) : <type>

<method> – the name of the action <parameters> – other objects required to perform the method  Any other objects required by the action are listed in parentheses (other1, other2...)  Empty parentheses () are used if the action doesn’t require other objects. <type> – the type of object that results from performing the action

<type> is the class of the object that results from the action. We use this term to distinguish it from the class of the object itself.

Methods may do much more than provide a result, creating some complexity. We will cover this in later lectures.

Just as we need an object to perform an action, we need an instance to a call (execute) a method:

Instances (Object) Calling (Executing) Methods

lecturers_device: iPhone Examples:

.owner: Person = Lecturer lecturers_device.check_time() .appstore_id: UserId = abc students_device.check_time() .check_time()

.check_time()

We call methods using an instance name followed by a dot and parentheses.

Instances and classes:  Instances are specific physical objects.  Classes are conceptual categories.  Every instance MUST belong to a class. Attributes:  Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.  The attributes define what it is to be a class. Methods:  Methods are actions that one can perform with an object. An instance is needed to call (execute) a method.  Methods are shared by all instances of a class.

Despite Apple’s best marketing effort, someone in this class may use a Galaxy phone:

Instances (Object) Class (Factory)

lecturers_device: iPhone iPhone

.owner: Person = Lecturer .owner : Person .appstore_id: UserId = abc .appstore_id: UserId

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall

students_device: Galaxy Galaxy

.owner: Person = Student .owner: Person .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId = def .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .powershare(EarBuds): None .powershare(EarBuds): None

iPhones and Galaxy smart phones share some attributes and methods, but also have some that are brand (class) specific.

Treating every object as having a distinct class is inefficient. People often understand things by organising them into a conceptual hierarchy.

iPhone Galaxy

.owner : Person .owner: Person .appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

iPhones and Galaxy phones are both types of smart phones. So, we can create a SmartPhone class.

We can move attributes and methods to the SmartPhone class and link our iPhone and Galaxy classes to it.

iPhone Galaxy

.owner : Person .owner: Person .appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

We can move attributes and methods to the SmartPhone class and link our iPhone and Galaxy classes to it.

iPhone Galaxy

.owner : Person .owner: Person .appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

We use an arrow to link a child class with specific functionality to a parent class with general functionality.

Each attribute and method appears once our hierarchy is cleaned.

iPhone Galaxy

.appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

Each attribute and method appears once our hierarchy is cleaned.

iPhone Galaxy

.appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

The hierarchy affects of class structure. Our instance get attributes and methods from their class and any parent class above.

iPhone Galaxy

.appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

Instances (Object) lecturers_device: iPhone students_device: Galaxy

.owner: Person = Lecturer .owner: Person = Student .appstore_id: UserId = abc .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId = def

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

Instances and classes:  Instances are specific physical objects.  Classes are conceptual categories.  Every instance MUST belong to a class.  Classes may form a hierarchy, where a child class is a specific form of a parent class. Attributes:  Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.  The attributes define what it is to be a class.  Instances get the attributes of their class and all their parent classes. Methods:  Methods are actions that one can perform with an object. An instance is needed to call (execute) a method.  Methods are shared by all instances of a class.  Instances get the methods of their class and all their parent classes.

Looking forward: Objects in Python  Objects in Python

Objects in Python parallel our example with Smart Phones:

iPhone Galaxy

.appstore_id: UserId .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId

.facetime(iPhone): VideoCall .powershare(EarBuds): None

Instances (Object) students_device: Galaxy lecturers_device: iPhone

.owner: Person = Student .owner: Person = Lecturer .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId = def .appstore_id: UserId = abc

.call(Phone): Conversation .call(Phone): Conversation .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite .tiktok(): None .tiktok(): None .powershare(EarBuds): None .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall

Objects in Python

Classes serve as factories. They define shared attributes and methods, and they are used to create instances:

Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class. Methods are actions that one can perform with an object shared by all instances of a class. Classes may form a hierarchy, where a child class is a specific form of a parent class. We use the class as a factory to make object instances.

Instances are specific objects stored in the computer’s memory.

Every instance MUST belong to a class. Instances get the attributes of their class and all their parent classes. Instances get the methods of their class and all their parent classes.

UML Diagrams

The figures we have been using are UML (Unified Modelling Language) diagrams:

<class>

.<attribute>: <type>

.<method>(<parameters>): <type>

<class_a> <class_b>

.<attribute_a>: <type> .<attribute_b>: <type>

.method_a(<parameters>): <type> .method_b(<parameters>): <type>

Instances (Object) object_2: <class_b> object_1: <class_a>

.<attribute>: <type> = <value> .<attribute>: <type> = <value> .<attribute_b>: <type> = <value> .<attribute_a>: <type> = <value>

.<method>(<parameters>): <type> .<method>(<parameters>): <type> .<method_b>(<parameters>): <type> .<method_a>(<parameters>): <type>

Python is an object-oriented language  An object is an instance of a class  Classes define methods and attributes shared by all their instances  Classes have a hierarchy • A child class inherits all the functionality of its parent class

Python lets us instruct a computer to perform tasks  The Python interpreter translates our code into machine-level operations

Python is an object-oriented language  An object is an instance of a class  Classes define methods and attributes shared by all their instances  Classes have a hierarchy • A child class inherits all the functionality of its parent class

Python lets us instruct a computer to perform tasks  The Python interpreter translates our code into machine-level operations PyCharm (IDE) is a development environment for writing and running Python code  Makes it easier to communicate with the Python interpreter  Includes many features we will explore later in the course

Python is an object-oriented language  An object is an instance of a class  Classes define methods and attributes shared by all their instances  Classes have a hierarchy • A child class inherits all the functionality of its parent class

Python lets us instruct a computer to perform tasks  The Python interpreter translates our code into machine-level operations PyCharm (IDE) is a development environment for writing and running Python code  Makes it easier to communicate with the Python interpreter  Includes many features we will explore later in the course  Python is now an essential tool for financial analysis and research  AI can help write and debug Python code  AI-generated code must always be tested and validated  A basic understanding of Python is still required to use AI effectively
